name: Sadhus (holy men)
shortDescription: Sadhus are Hindu holy men who renounce worldly life to pursue spiritual liberation (moksha). They are often wandering ascetics, dedicated to meditation and devotion.
description: Sadhus are Hindu ascetics who have renounced worldly life in pursuit of spiritual liberation (moksha). The term 'Sadhu' comes from the Sanskrit word 'sādhu,' meaning 'good man,' 'holy man,' or 'one who practices sadhana' (spiritual practice). Sadhus are a diverse group, encompassing various sects and traditions within Hinduism, including Shaivism (devotees of Shiva), Vaishnavism (devotees of Vishnu), and Shaktism (devotees of Shakti). Their practices vary, ranging from intense meditation and yoga to devotional singing and wandering pilgrimage. Many Sadhus live a life of extreme austerity, foregoing material possessions and comforts. They often wear saffron-colored robes, symbolizing renunciation, and may smear their bodies with ash, a reminder of the impermanence of life. Some Sadhus engage in practices that may seem extreme to outsiders, such as self-mortification or prolonged periods of silence. Their goal is to transcend the ego and achieve union with the divine. Sadhus are considered to be living embodiments of spiritual ideals and are often revered by Hindu society. They play an important role in preserving and transmitting Hindu traditions and wisdom. The lifestyle of a Sadhu represents a complete dedication to the pursuit of spiritual enlightenment, a path that requires immense discipline, faith, and detachment from the material world. Their presence in places like Varanasi is particularly significant, as the city is considered a sacred space conducive to spiritual liberation.

sources.title: Asceticism and Eroticism in the Mythology of Siva
sources.author: Wendy Doniger O'Flaherty
